checks.a_analysis: I examined each text element in the output against the source slides. The headings and body text for the title slide, survey background, solicitation, summary, and contact information precisely match the source material. No hallucinated or invented text was found in the provided sections. Note that the meta-comment '11 sections omitted for token budget' is a structural placeholder in the JSON and not a fabricated text element within the document content itself.

I verified the email addresses on slide 16 against the contact elements in the JSON. DABrannan@cabarruscounty.us, anna.verrill@nc.gov, and tim.johnson@nc.gov are all present and identical to the source. No URLs or phone numbers were present in the source to check.

The output JSON is missing a massive portion of the document. Slides 4 through 14, which contain the actual survey results (Q1 through Q10) including all charts and qualitative feedback, are entirely absent from the 'sections' array. The jump in reading order indices from 45 to 268 confirms this large gap. The placeholder '11 sections omitted for token budget' explicitly acknowledges the missing pages.
checks.b_evidence_source: Source PDF pages 4-14 containing survey results for Q1-Q10, including multiple charts and text blocks.
checks.b_evidence_output: The entire content of these 11 pages is missing, replaced by the placeholder: {"_omitted": "11 sections omitted for token budget"}.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections array, between reading order 45 and 268 (pages 4-14)

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains lists and charts but no formal data tables. The lists are preserved correctly in the sections that were converted.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: For the slides that were converted (1, 2, 3, 15, and 16), the text is preserved completely without significant internal omissions. The major omission is of entire sections (pages), which is handled under check C3.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: I performed a word-for-word comparison of the text in the title, background, and summary slides. The output faithfully represents the source text, including minor typos present in the original (e.g., '52 response' instead of 'responses' on page 2). Reformatting of bullet points into newline-separated text within paragraphs is an acceptable accessibility normalization.

checks.a_analysis: The document structure was reviewed for repeated content. Each slide's title and content appear exactly once in the output, mirroring the source PDF sequence without duplication.

The heading hierarchy is well-maintained and normalized for accessibility. Major slide titles are mapped to Heading Level 1. On page 16, sub-headers like 'LGC Chair' are correctly nested as Heading Level 2 under the main 'Contacts' header.
The source document does not contain any formal data tables; it uses charts (which are treated as images) and text lists. Therefore, no table corruption is possible.
The reading order in the JSON output follows the logical sequence of the presentation slides (1 through 16) and the top-to-bottom layout of each slide.

Several bulleted lists in the source document were not converted into structured list elements (list and list_item types). On page 2, the entire list is merged into a single paragraph with manual bullet characters ('*'). On pages 3 and 15, list items are rendered as individual paragraphs instead of a cohesive list structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 2 contains a bulleted list: 'Following last GICC Meeting...', 'LGC and SGUC drafted 10 survey questions', etc.
checks.b_evidence_output: The list items are merged into a single paragraph: "Following last GICC Meeting,\n* LGC and SGUC drafted 10 survey questions\n * Type of census-related data used..."

checks.a_analysis: The email addresses on page 16 are present as plain text but were not identified as clickable mailto links in the structured output, which is a requirement for accessibility fidelity when URLs/emails are clearly visible in the source.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully translates the slide-based organization of the original PDF into a single-column responsive layout. Each slide is treated as a distinct section, maintaining the logical flow of information (from Slide 1 through Slide 16). While visual styling like fonts and the exact placement of logos differs, the organization of content matches the source's intent.
There are no data tables present in the source PDF. Data is presented through bulleted lists and charts, which are handled appropriately in the HTML output.

All meaningful graphical content from the PDF is present in the converted document. This includes the agency logo (NCDIT) and the critical bar and pie charts depicting survey responses found on slides 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10.

The document contains significant visual duplication of content. First, slide titles are frequently repeated within their respective sections (e.g., appearing as both a heading and a paragraph below charts on slides 4 and 5). Second, and more importantly, the entire textual content of the 16-slide presentation is repeated in its entirety in a concatenated block at the end of the document, appearing after the 'Contacts' section. This represents a complete repetition of the document's body content.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains content distributed once across 16 slides.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows the entire content of the presentation repeated in a single block at the bottom of the page (beginning with 'Local Government Committee' and ending with 'tim.johnson@nc.gov') after the slide-by-slide sections conclude.
checks.b_evidence_location: The final section of the HTML document, following the Slide 16 ('Contacts') content.
